notes.note: The praetorship was the senior magistracy below the consulship, normally sought in a candidate's appointed year; Cicero urges Furnius not to abandon his command in Gaul merely to be in Rome for the elections.
Candidates for magistracies had to canvass and stand in person at Rome (the comitia); Furnius's military service in Gaul kept him from the city during the campaigning season.

notes.anchor: had you been aedile, your year would come two years after
notes.note: Refers to the cursus honorum: a fixed two-year interval (biennium) was required between holding the aedileship and standing for the praetorship. Furnius had not been aedile, so no such designated year bound him.
Plancus was consul-designate for 42 BC; a candidate's prospects were brighter when a friendly consul presided over the elections and could lend support.
Quintus Tullius Cicero, the orator's younger brother, praetor 62 BC and a former legate of Caesar in Gaul; both brothers would be proscribed and killed by the triumvirs later in 43 BC.
A libertus (freedman) of Furnius, present at the meeting as a trusted member of the household; freedmen commonly served their former masters as agents and messengers.
